focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Doug got to Worthington twenty minutes early because he always got anywhere twenty minutes early. It was a claims habit, left over from years of showing up first to assess the site, note the conditions before anyone else's presence altered them. Kelsey had picked the diner on High Street — neutral ground, she'd said, which implied that Doug's apartment and her apartment and every restaurant they'd ever been to together were charged territory now, places where the toast might follow them in. He'd agreed because he agreed with most things Kelsey suggested, and because the last time he'd disagreed with her about a restaurant she'd been fourteen and the argument had been about Applebee's and he'd lost.

The conditions here were a half-full diner with vinyl booths, a specials board in faded chalk that listed a turkey melt and a soup that was either cream of mushroom or cream of chicken depending on how you read the handwriting, and a draft coming under the front door that carried the smell of road salt and wet asphalt. February in Columbus. Not brutal, just relentless — a cold that didn't argue with you, just waited.

He chose a booth near the back, away from the window, which turned out poorly because the kitchen door swung open every thirty seconds and he could hear the fryer pop and plates being stacked and a cook saying something muffled and laughing at it alone. He ordered coffee. The waitress was a woman around his age, heavyset, with a brace on her left wrist and a pen tucked behind each ear — both blue, like she'd been burned once by running out of ink and had decided it wasn't going to happen again. She brought the coffee in a mug that said OHIO IS FOR LOVERS, which was wrong about Ohio in at least two ways Doug could think of.

He wrapped his hands aro...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the steps and stopped at his side of the fence.

"It happened fast," he said to Doug, not to Margaret. "I went out there with a flashlight and it was already split open."

"Had you noticed any bowing in the wall before February eighth?"

Carl looked at the collapsed ring of the pool. "Maybe a little by the ladder. I don't know. Pools always look a little wrong in winter."

Margaret made a sound through her nose and looked past Carl at the dead roses.

Doug took photos of the seam, the fence line, the dead plants, the trellis base, the stripe the water had cut through the yard. Halfway through the second set his phone buzzed in his coat pocket. He checked the screen without thinking. A 917 number. New York.

He put the phone back in his pocket and kept shooting.

When he was done, Margaret said, "So what happens now?"

Doug lowered the camera. "Now I write it up tonight and send it to my supervisor for legal review in the morning. Nobody gets to deny it off two phone calls."

Margaret nodded once. Carl shifted the hose from one hand to the other and said nothing.

The apartment was dark when Doug got home. He turned on the kitchen light, poured a bowl of Cheerios, and ate standing at the counter while the camera uploaded the photos. The milk was close to its sell-by date but still fine. Mud had dried in a thin line along the hem of his pants. The second bedroom door stayed closed.

He set the bowl in the sink, opened a blank email to Janet, and typed the subject line: BURRIS/COMMONWEALTH — timeline, site photos, request for legal review. Halfway through the first paragraph his phone lit on the counter. Same 917 number. He watched it ring until the screen went dark. Then he went back to the timeline, starting again with the crack Carl Burris said he had heard in the dark.
